nodes.label: P5.5 — calibrated early-exit on the continual home: adaptive exit dominated -> ship a fixed reader (STOP①)
nodes.rationale: The calibrated max-softmax exit is Pareto-dominated on the P4.5 class-incremental stream (0.479 acc at 74.7k, worst of five readers); the inversion of P5.4 — on the flat continual home per-depth heads are weak-but-decorrelated so pooling (all-tap) beats any single depth; ship truncation L2-3 (8x cheaper, -0.07 acc) or all-tap; adaptive exit STRUCK.

nodes.label: P5.5 verdict: the adaptive calibrated exit is Pareto-dominated and STRUCK (STOP① not passed)
nodes.rationale: On the P4.5 continual class-incremental stream, the calibrated max-softmax exit (one-shot and per-task-refit tau) is dominated by both fixed readers (0.479 acc at 74.7k cost, worst accuracy at high cost); the calibration collapses to grid extremes (exit-L1 or exit-the-decayed-L12) because no single depth can meet the 95%-of-all-tap CALM bar by construction on this task.

nodes.label: The unifying law: single-depth placement wins where one depth is sharply best; pooling wins where signal is weak-but-decorrelated
nodes.rationale: The inversion of P5.4: on static headroom (depth pays) late layers drift so all-tap is diluted and one sharp head wins; on the continual flat home (depth doesn't pay) per-depth heads are weak-but-decorrelated (oracle 0.87 >> any single 0.60) so pooling (all-tap 0.62) beats every single depth by construction — ties P5.4 and P5.5 into one mechanism.
